levels.title: Learn pico-celery
levels.description: Background task processing with dependency injection.

levels.title: Worker vs Client Architecture
levels.description:
How Celery Works

Celery splits work into two roles: clients dispatch tasks, and workers execute them. A message broker (Redis or RabbitMQ) sits in between, and results are stored in a result backend.

Client  ──>  Broker  ──>  Worker  ──>  Result Backend

The client calls send_task("add", args=[2, 3]). The broker queues the message. A worker picks it up, runs the function, and stores the result.

What pico-celery Adds

pico-celery integrates Celery with pico-ioc dependency injection. Instead of plain functions, your tasks live inside component classes with full DI support.

  • @task marks async methods on worker-side component classes.
  • @send_task declares client-side stubs that dispatch tasks by name.
  • The @celery class decorator marks a client class.

Workers get their dependencies injected just like any other pico-ioc component. Clients never execute task logic — they only send messages to the broker.

levels.title: Defining Tasks with @task
levels.description:
Adding Task Decorators

Worker-side task classes use the @task decorator on their async methods. Each @task needs a name argument that uniquely identifies the task across the system.

The MathTasks class below is already a @component, but its methods are missing @task decorators. Add them so the metadata is registered correctly.

The test verifies that each method has _pico_celery_method_meta with the correct task name.

files.name: tasks.py
files.content:
from pico_ioc import component
from pico_celery import task

@component
class MathTasks:
    # 👈 Add @task(name="math.add")
    async def add(self, x: int, y: int) -> int:
        return x + y

    # 👈 Add @task(name="math.multiply")
    async def multiply(self, x: int, y: int) -> int:
        return x * y

files.name: test_tasks.py
files.content:
from tasks import MathTasks

def test_add_has_task_meta():
    meta = getattr(MathTasks.add, '_pico_celery_method_meta', None)
    assert meta is not None, "add() is missing @task decorator"
    assert meta.name == "math.add"

def test_multiply_has_task_meta():
    meta = getattr(MathTasks.multiply, '_pico_celery_method_meta', None)
    assert meta is not None, "multiply() is missing @task decorator"
    assert meta.name == "math.multiply"

hints: Import `task` from `pico_celery` — it is already imported for you.
Place `@task(name="math.add")` directly above the `async def add(...)` method.

levels.title: Client Declarations with @send_task
levels.description:
Building a Task Client

Client classes declare which tasks they can dispatch using @send_task. The class itself must be decorated with @celery so pico-celery knows it is a client.

Method bodies in a client are never executed — they only exist to declare the signature. Use pass as the body.

Add the @celery class decorator and @send_task method decorators below.

files.name: client.py
files.content:
from pico_celery import celery, send_task

# 👈 Add @celery class decorator
class MathClient:
    # 👈 Add @send_task(name="math.add")
    async def add(self, x: int, y: int) -> int:
        pass

    # 👈 Add @send_task(name="math.multiply")
    async def multiply(self, x: int, y: int) -> int:
        pass

files.name: test_client.py
files.content:
from client import MathClient

def test_class_has_celery_decorator():
    assert getattr(MathClient, '_pico_celery_meta', None) is not None, \
        "MathClient is missing @celery class decorator"

def test_add_has_send_task_meta():
    meta = getattr(MathClient.add, '_pico_celery_method_meta', None)
    assert meta is not None, "add() is missing @send_task decorator"
    assert meta.name == "math.add"

def test_multiply_has_send_task_meta():
    meta = getattr(MathClient.multiply, '_pico_celery_method_meta', None)
    assert meta is not None, "multiply() is missing @send_task decorator"
    assert meta.name == "math.multiply"

hints: The `@celery` decorator goes on the class itself, above `class MathClient:`.
`@send_task(name="math.add")` goes above the `async def add(...)` method.
Repeat for `multiply` with `@send_task(name="math.multiply")`. The method bodies stay as `pass`.

levels.title: Configuration and Deployment
levels.description:
CelerySettings

pico-celery reads its configuration from CelerySettings, which you can provide via environment variables or a settings file:

from pico_celery import CelerySettings

# These are the key fields:
# broker_url = "redis://localhost:6379/0"
# backend_url = "redis://localhost:6379/1"

Docker Compose Example

A typical deployment uses Redis as both broker and backend, with separate containers for the worker and client:

services:
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"

  worker:
    build: .
    command: pico-celery worker
    environment:
      - CELERY_BROKER_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
      - CELERY_BACKEND_URL=redis://redis:6379/1
    depends_on:
      - redis

  client:
    build: .
    command: python main.py
    environment:
      - CELERY_BROKER_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
      - CELERY_BACKEND_URL=redis://redis:6379/1
    depends_on:
      - redis

Auto-Discovery

When you use pico-boot, it automatically discovers pico-celery as a plugin and registers all @task and @celery components. For manual setup, pass your task modules to pico_ioc.init() as usual.

levels.title: Task with Dependency Injection
levels.description:
Injecting Services into Tasks

pico-celery tasks are regular pico-ioc components, so they get full dependency injection. The EmailTasks class below depends on a TemplateService via its constructor.

However, TemplateService is missing its @component decorator, so pico-ioc cannot inject it. Add the decorator and verify the DI wiring works.

files.name: tasks.py
test_tasks.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import tasks

def test_email_tasks_has_template_service():
    pico.init(modules=[tasks])
    email_tasks = pico.get(tasks.EmailTasks)
    assert email_tasks.template_service is not None, \
        "TemplateService was not injected"
    assert isinstance(email_tasks.template_service, tasks.TemplateService)

def test_template_service_renders():
    pico.init(modules=[tasks])
    ts = pico.get(tasks.TemplateService)
    result = ts.render("welcome", email="test@example.com")
    assert "welcome" in result
    assert "test@example.com" in result

hints: The `TemplateService` class needs to be registered with pico-ioc so it can be injected.
Add `@component` from `pico_ioc` above the `class TemplateService:` line.
Once `TemplateService` is a component, pico-ioc will automatically inject it into `EmailTasks` via the constructor.
